analogy: Think of "Minerals and Rocks" through the chapter's own opening images: LANDFORMS This unit deals with • Landforms and their evolution • Geomorphic processes — weathering, mass wasting, erosion and deposition; soils — formation UNIT III GEOMORPHIC PROCESSES C H A P T E R After learning about how the earth was born, how it evolved... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: LANDFORMS, GEOMORPHIC PROCESSES, ENDOGENIC PROCESSES, GEOMORPHIC PROCESSES 39. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Minerals and Rocks is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that lANDFORMS This unit deals with • Landforms and their evolution • Geomorphic processes — weathering, mass wasting, erosion and deposition; soils — formation UNIT III

The text explains that gEOMORPHIC PROCESSES C H A P T E R After learning about how the earth was born, how it evolved its crust and other inner layers, how its crustal plates moved and are moving, and other information on earthquakes, the forms of volcanism and about the rocks and minerals the crust is composed of, it is time to know in detail about the surface of the earth on which we live.

The text explains that you are well aware that it has moved and moves vertically and horizontally.

The text explains that of course, it moved a bit faster in the past than the rate at which it is moving now.

The text explains that the differences in the internal forces operating from within the earth which built up the crust have been responsible for the variations in the outer surface of the crust.

The text explains that the earth’s surface is being continuously subjected to external forces induced basically by energy (sunlight).

The text explains that of course, the internal forces are still active though with different intensities.

The text explains that that means, the earth’s surface is being continuously subjected to by external forces originating within the earth’s atmosphere and by internal forces from within the earth.

The text explains that the external forces are known as exogenic forces and the internal forces are known as endogenic forces.

The text explains that the actions of exogenic forces result in wearing down (degradation) of relief/elevations and filling up (aggradation) of basins/ depressions, on the earth’s surface.

The text explains that the phenomenon of wearing down of relief variations of the surface of the earth through erosion is known as gradation.

The text explains that the endogenic forces continuously elevate or build up parts of the earth’s surface and hence the exogenic processes fail to even out the relief variations of the surface of the earth.

The text explains that so, variations remain as long as the opposing actions of exogenic and endogenic forces continue.

The text explains that in general terms, the endogenic forces are mainly land building forces and the exogenic processes are mainly land wearing forces.

The text explains that humans depend on it for their sustenance and have been using it extensively and intensively.

The text explains that so, it is essential to understand its nature in order to use it effectively without disturbing its balance and diminishing its potential for the future.

The text explains that almost all organisms contribute to sustain the earth’s environment.

The text explains that however, humans have caused extensive damage to the environment through over use of resources.

The text explains that most of the surface of the earth had and has been shaped over very long periods of time (hundreds and thousands of years) and because of its use and misuse by humans its potential is being diminished at a fast rate.

The text explains that if the processes which shaped and are shaping the surface of the earth into varieties of forms (shapes) and the nature of materials of which it is composed of, are understood, precautions can be taken to minimise the detrimental effects of human use and to preserve it for posterity.

The text explains that gEOMORPHIC PROCESSES You would like to know the meaning of geomorphic processes.

The text explains that the endogenic and exogenic forces causing physical stresses and chemical actions on earth materials and

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 38 bringing about changes in the configuration of the surface of the earth are known as geomorphic processes.

The text explains that diastrophism and volcanism are endogenic geomorphic processes.

The text explains that weathering, mass wasting, erosion and deposition are exogenic geomorphic processes.

The text explains that these exogenic processes are dealt with in detail in this chapter.

The text explains that any exogenic element of nature (like water, ice, wind, etc.,) capable of acquiring and transporting earth materials can be called a geomorphic agent.

The text explains that geomorphic processes and geomorphic agents especially exogenic, unless stated separately, are one and the same.

The text explains that a process is a force applied on earth materials affecting the same.

The text explains that an agent is a mobile medium (like running water, moving ice masses, wind, waves and currents etc.) which removes, transports and deposits earth materials.

The text explains that running water, groundwater, glaciers, wind, waves and currents, etc., can be called geomorphic agents.

The text explains that do you think it is essential to distinguish geomorphic agents and geomorphic processes?

The text explains that gravity besides being a directional force activating all downslope movements of matter also causes stresses on the earth’s materials.

The text explains that indirect gravitational stresses activate wave and tide induced currents and winds.

The text explains that so, gravitational stresses are as important as the other geomorphic processes.

The text explains that gravity is the force that is keeping us in contact with the surface and it is the force that switches on the movement of all surface material on earth.

The text explains that all the movements either within the earth or on the surface of the earth occur due to gradients — from higher levels to lower levels, from high pressure to low pressure areas etc.

The text explains that eNDOGENIC PROCESSES The energy emanating from within the earth is the main force behind endogenic geomorphic processes.

The text explains that this energy is mostly generated by radioactivity, rotational and tidal friction and primordial heat from the origin of the earth.

The text explains that this energy due to geothermal gradients and heat flow from within induces diastrophism and volcanism in the lithosphere.

The text explains that due to variations in geothermal gradients and heat flow from within, crustal thickness and strength, the action of endogenic forces are not uniform and hence the tectonically controlled original crustal surface is uneven.

The text explains that diastrophism All processes that move, elevate or build up portions of the earth’s crust come under diastrophism.

The text explains that they include: (i) orogenic processes involving mountain building through severe folding and affecting long and narrow belts of the earth’s crust; (ii) epeirogenic processes involving uplift or warping of large parts of the earth’s crust; (iii) earthquakes involving local relatively minor movements; (iv) plate tectonics involving horizontal movements of crustal plates.

The text explains that (a) Granite (c) Quartz (b) Clay (d) Salts (iii) Debris avalanche can be included in the category of: (a) Landslides (c) Rapid flow mass movements (b) Slow flow mass movements (d) Subsidence 2.

The text explains that (i) It is weathering that is responsible for bio-diversity on the earth.

The text explains that (ii) What are mass movements that are real rapid and perceptible?

The text explains that (iii) What are the various mobile and mighty exogenic geomorphic agents and what is the prime job they perform?

The text explains that (iv) Is weathering essential as a pre-requisite in the formation of soils?

The text explains that (i) “Our earth is a playfield for two opposing groups of geomorphic processes.” Discuss.

The text explains that (ii) Exogenic geomorphic processes derive their ultimate energy from the sun’s heat.

The text explains that (iii) Are physical and chemical weathering processes independent of each other?

The text explains that (iv) How do you distinguish between the process of soil formation and soil- forming factors?

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 5: Minerals and Rocks

cases.name: Diastrophism All
cases.point: Diastrophism All processes that move, elevate or build up portions of the earth’s crust come under diastrophism. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch5 pg 2)

cases.name: Volcanism Volcanism
cases.point: Volcanism Volcanism includes the movement of molten rock (magma) onto or toward the earth’s surface and also formation of many intrusive and extrusive volcanic forms. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch5 pg 2)

cases.point: Chemical Weathering Processes A group of weathering processes viz; solution, carbonation, hydration, oxidation and reduction act on the rocks to decompose, dissolve or reduce them to... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch5 pg 4)

cases.name: Physical Weathering Processes Physical
cases.point: Physical Weathering Processes Physical or mechanical weathering processes depend on some applied forces. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch5 pg 4)

schemes.name: There
schemes.point: There are a number of processes within weathering which act either individually or together to affect the earth materials in order to reduce them to fragmental... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch5 pg 4)

schemes.name: Chemical Weathering Processes
schemes.point: Chemical Weathering Processes A group of weathering processes viz; solution, carbonation, hydration, oxidation and reduction act on the rocks to decompose, dissolve or reduce them to... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch5 pg 4)

schemes.name: The
schemes.point: The same erosional agents viz., running water, glaciers, wind, waves and groundwater act as aggradational or depositional agents also. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch5 pg 7)

schemes.name: In
schemes.point: In fact soil forming factors act in union and affect the action of one another. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch5 pg 8)

schemes.name: Institutional reference
schemes.point: A soil becomes mature when all soil-forming processes act for a sufficiently long time developing a profile. (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch5 pg 9)

prelims_traps: Do not treat LANDFORMS and GEOMORPHIC PROCESSES as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Minerals and Rocks; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use LANDFORMS and GEOMORPHIC PROCESSES to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
